content.assessment: A transparency and evidence infrastructure does not itself alter geographic access, capacity, or transport arrangements, but it directly supports better-informed network planning under any operational model by generating Hungary-specific data where current evidence is contested. This is particularly relevant given that international findings on closure-depopulation linkage are explicitly contested and cannot be assumed to generalize to Hungary. However, as a lens-specific matter, an evidence registry has no direct mechanism to resolve existing geographic or transport constraints; it can only make their consequences more visible over time.
opportunities: A registry could quantify how documented rural transport gaps and informal inter-municipal competition dynamics actually affect network outcomes over time, informing more precise future interventions (F-live-school_network_planning-07, F-live-school_network_planning-09)
strengths: Directly responds to the contested state of closure-depopulation evidence by proposing a Hungary-specific registry rather than relying on non-replicated foreign findings (F-live-school_network_planning-06)
Could systematically track geographic/transport cost data that is currently under-documented in closure proposals, improving future network-planning decisions
threats: Absent binding decision rules, purely informational infrastructure risks being circumvented by discretionary administrative practice, consistent with the historical pattern of ad hoc closure decisions in this network (F-live-school_network_planning-04)
weaknesses: As an information-only mechanism, it does not by itself deliver any change in geographic access, capacity, or transport provision; it can only improve the evidentiary basis for decisions made under other proposals
